message:
beads: file gh7gt (fcp-raptorq encode ESI overflow, LOW)

File flywheel_connectors-gh7gt documenting the defensive-depth gap in
encode_all / into_encode_all where k_prime + i was computed with raw
u32 addition. Not currently exploitable — config bounds keep the sum
well under u32::MAX — but inconsistent with the saturating/checked
arithmetic discipline used elsewhere in the crate. The fix itself
already landed in c3cc8695; this commit only syncs the bead log so
br sees the filing.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-abnue","title":"fuzz/session: target decodes hello/ack/cookie bytes without invariants","description":"File: fuzz/fuzz_targets/session.rs:6-9

Finding: the fuzz body only calls `decode_hello_cbor(data)`, `decode_ack_cbor(data)`, and `decode_cookie_bytes(data)` and discards every result.

Why this matters: this only checks for panics. It never asserts transcript/session invariants when one or more decoders succeed, so semantic parser bugs can survive.

Suggested follow-up: when hello or ack decoding succeeds, assert stable transcript derivation / suite constraints / session-id shape rather than parse-and-drop only.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:19:47.550495Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:19:47.550495Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-abwt","title":"TEST: Canonical Serialization Unit Tests (Deterministic CBOR, Schema Hash, CDDL Validation)","description":"# TEST: Canonical Serialization Unit Tests

## Goal
Prove that serialization is deterministic and schema-bound.

## Test Categories

### Deterministic CBOR Encoding
- [ ] Same logical object → same bytes (always)
- [ ] Map key ordering is canonical (sorted)
- [ ] Integer encoding is minimal
- [ ] Float encoding is canonical
- [ ] No duplicate map keys
- [ ] Definite length encoding used

### Schema Hash Binding
- [ ] SchemaId (namespace, name, version) hashes correctly
- [ ] SchemaHash is 32 bytes (BLAKE3)
- [ ] Payload format: schema_hash || canonical_cbor_bytes
- [ ] Schema hash mismatch detected on decode

### Schema Validation
- [ ] Valid object passes schema check
- [ ] Missing required field rejected
- [ ] Wrong field type rejected
- [ ] Extra fields handled per policy (reject or ignore)
- [ ] Nested object validation works

### CDDL Compliance
- [ ] All FCP2 objects have CDDL definitions
- [ ] Generated bytes match CDDL constraints
- [ ] CDDL can be used for cross-language validation

### Roundtrip Tests
- [ ] Encode → decode → encode produces identical bytes
- [ ] All core object types roundtrip correctly
- [ ] Large objects roundtrip correctly
- [ ] Nested objects roundtrip correctly

### Bounds and Limits
- [ ] Oversized objects rejected at encode time
- [ ] Deeply nested objects bounded
- [ ] String length limits enforced
- [ ] Array length limits enforced

### Decode Safety
- [ ] Malformed CBOR rejected without panic
- [ ] Truncated input rejected
- [ ] Oversized length prefix rejected (DoS prevention)
- [ ] Recursive structure depth bounded

## Golden Vectors
- Each core object type with canonical bytes
- Schema hash computation examples
- Malformed inputs that must be rejected

## Property Tests
- Determinism: same input → same output
- Commutativity with schema: schema_hash(decode(encode(x))) == schema_hash(x)

## Fuzz Targets
- CBOR decode with arbitrary bytes
- Schema validation with malformed objects

## Logging Requirements
```json
{
  "test_name": "...",
  "object_type": "...",
  "schema_id": "...",
  "operation": "encode|decode|validate",
  "result": "pass|fail",
  "reason_code": "..."
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- All core types have golden vectors
- Determinism verified by property tests
- Fuzz targets find no panics

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-g2c","title":"fcp.airtable: Full CRUD operations on records","description":"# fcp.airtable: Full CRUD Operations on Records

## Goal
Implement record CRUD with batching, pagination, and strict safety controls.

## External surface (NetworkConstraints)
Default deny.
- Network egress MUST be restricted per-operation via manifest `NetworkConstraints`.
- Allow only the configured service host(s); HTTPS-only where applicable; deny redirects by default.
- Bound timeouts + response sizes; treat all remote responses as untrusted input; never leak secrets/PII in logs or errors.

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Recommended deployment pattern: run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies.

## Scope
Operations (representative):
- `airtable.records.list` (pagination, filter/sort)
- `airtable.records.get`
- `airtable.records.create` / `airtable.records.create_batch`
- `airtable.records.update` / `airtable.records.update_batch`
- `airtable.records.upsert`
- `airtable.records.delete` / `airtable.records.delete_batch` (Dangerous)

## Key requirements
- Capability gated:
  - read vs write vs delete are separate capabilities
- Rate limiting and backpressure are mechanical.
- Pagination:
  - support continuation tokens
  - bound page sizes
- Batch operations:
  - handle partial failures deterministically
  - stable error mapping per record
- Idempotency / retries:
  - upsert is preferred for retry-safe workflows
  - deletes and schema writes must be treated as Dangerous (policy + receipts)

## Tests
- Mock-only tests cover:
  - pagination edge cases
  - batch partial failure semantics
  - upsert conflict handling
  - redaction: never log record bodies by default

## Acceptance Criteria
- CRUD operations behave deterministically under retries.
- Dangerous operations are properly gated and audited.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-g37n0","title":"fcp-slack: ok=true responses can panic on missing payload data","description":"## Finding
`SlackApiResponse<T>` stores the flattened success payload as `Option<T>` ([connectors/slack/src/types.rs:123-129](connectors/slack/src/types.rs:123)). `check_response()` only checks `ok` ([connectors/slack/src/client.rs:447-458](connectors/slack/src/client.rs:447)). Multiple client paths then call `resp.data.expect("ok response has data")` ([connectors/slack/src/client.rs:162](connectors/slack/src/client.rs:162), [209](connectors/slack/src/client.rs:209), [228](connectors/slack/src/client.rs:228), [238](connectors/slack/src/client.rs:238), [257](connectors/slack/src/client.rs:257), [270](connectors/slack/src/client.rs:270), [282](connectors/slack/src/client.rs:282), [302](connectors/slack/src/client.rs:302), [312](connectors/slack/src/client.rs:312)).

A malformed or partial `ok:true` body therefore becomes a process panic instead of an `FcpError`/`SlackError`. The crate already documents the `None` case in tests (`slack_api_response_error_has_no_data`), so this is not theoretical parser behavior.

## Repro shape
Return `{"ok":true}` or `{"ok":true,"channel":"C1"}` from `chat.postMessage`. `check_response()` accepts it and the subsequent `.expect(...)` aborts the connector.

## Recommendation
Replace each `.expect("ok response has data")` with an error-mapped `ok_or_else(...)` path that preserves the operation context and treats malformed success envelopes as API/protocol errors, not panics.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:56:43.789357Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:23:29.875248Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:23:29.872787Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2811,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-g37n0","author":"jemanuel","text":"Fixed in 2dd92d08. Root cause: 9 client paths called .expect("ok response has data") on a flattened Option<T> payload — a partial {"ok":true} envelope therefore panicked the connector instead of surfacing as an error. Fix: added Self::expect_data(data, method) helper that maps None to a terminal SlackError::Api { ok: true, error: "Slack API method \`X\` returned ok=true with no payload", .. }, and migrated all 9 call sites through it. The ok:true discriminator preserves the partial-envelope case as distinct from classic ok=false errors. Regression test ok_true_with_missing_payload_is_mapped_to_api_error_not_panic asserts the method name and 'ok=true' phrase appear in the error, and that to_fcp_error() maps to FcpError::External. Verified: 210 lib tests + 51/52 integration tests pass (the one pre-existing failure list_channels_happy_path is an unrelated URL-encoding bug confirmed by bisect).","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:23:29Z"}]}

+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-gh0j7","title":"fuzz/fcps_frame: target only decodes frames/symbols without checking accepted-state invariants","description":"File: fuzz/fuzz_targets/fcps_frame.rs:18-37

Finding: the fuzz body only calls `FcpsFrameHeader::decode`, `FcpsFrame::decode`, `SymbolRecord::decode`, and `FrameFlags::from_bits_truncate`, then drops all successful results.

Why this matters: the target is good for panic discovery but weak for semantic coverage. It never checks that successfully decoded frames and symbol records obey internal size/header invariants.

Suggested follow-up: when decode succeeds, assert header/frame consistency, record bounds consistency, and stable behavior across repeated decode paths.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:20:10.749754Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:20:10.749754Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-gh7gt","title":"fcp-raptorq: encode_all/into_encode_all ESI arithmetic uses wrapping add, not checked_add","description":"In `crates/fcp-raptorq/src/encode.rs:141-144` (`encode_all`) and `crates/fcp-raptorq/src/encode.rs:162-166` (`into_encode_all`), the repair symbol ESI is computed as `k_prime + i` where both are u32. In debug builds, overflow panics; in release, it wraps into the source-symbol ESI range and silently collides with existing source symbols.

**Bounded in practice.** `config.repair_symbols(source_symbols)` saturates to `u32::MAX`, but `source_symbols` comes from `payload_len.div_ceil(symbol_size)` where `payload_len ≤ config.max_object_size` (typically 64 MiB) and `symbol_size >= 1`. So `source_symbols ≤ 64 MiB`, well under u32::MAX. The current config bounds keep the arithmetic safe.

**Brittle.** The rest of the crate uses `saturating_add` / `checked_add` consistently (see `config.rs:155`, `decode.rs:585`, `decode.rs:598`). `encode.rs:142` and `encode.rs:164` are the only sites that use raw `+` on user-derived-ish arithmetic with no bound check.

**Suggested fix**: `let esi = k_prime.checked_add(i).ok_or(EncodeError::SymbolCountOverflow)?;` (or saturating_add). Mirror the `decode.rs:585` pattern.

Severity: LOW. Not currently exploitable; filed as a defensive-depth bead to keep the saturating/checked arithmetic discipline uniform across the crate.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:14:51.293708Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:14:51.293708Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-gif","title":"fcp.pinecone: Pinecone Vector Database Connector","description":"# fcp.pinecone - Pinecone Vector Database Connector (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide an FCP2-compliant connector for Pinecone (managed vector DB) supporting vector storage and semantic retrieval under strict zone isolation.

## Manifest archetypes (closed set)
- `storage`
- `knowledge`

## Zone model
Single-zone bound: one connector instance ↔ one `ZoneId`.

## External surface
### NetworkConstraints
Default-deny; allow only the configured Pinecone endpoint(s) and port(s) required by declared operations.

### Credential injection
- API key via `CredentialId`.
- Never persist or log keys.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `pinecone.index.manage`
- `pinecone.vectors.read`
- `pinecone.vectors.write`
- `pinecone.search.query`

## Operations (initial)
- index: create/describe/list/delete
- vectors: upsert/fetch/delete/query

## Tests
- Mock-only unit tests with wiremock.
- E2E compliance run validates default deny + NetworkConstraints + receipts for writes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Passes connector compliance runner.
- Deterministic tests (no live Pinecone calls).

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-gz2y","title":"[IDEA-27] Connector State Fork Resolution Workflow","description":"## Background
Stateful connectors must be single-writer; forks are safety incidents. We need detection and a structured resolution workflow.

## Goals
- Detect forked ConnectorStateObject chains.
- Pause connector execution and emit audit evidence.
- Provide tooling to resolve/merge or choose a head.

## Non-goals
- Automatic conflict resolution without operator input.

## Approach
- Add fork detection in state manager (same prev, divergent heads).
- Emit `ConnectorStateFork` audit event.
- Add CLI `fcp connector state resolve` to select resolution strategy.

## Risks
- Incorrect resolution could lose state; require explicit approval.

## Success Criteria
- Forks are detected deterministically and block further writes.
- Resolution workflow is auditable and reversible.

## Acceptance Criteria (Refined)
- Fork detection pauses connector writes immediately.
- Resolution emits audit event referencing chosen head.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-h1wi","title":"TEST: Connector Performance Benchmark Suite","description":"# TEST: Connector Performance Benchmark Suite (FCP2)


## Goal
Establish a benchmark suite to measure and guard connector performance budgets (latency, memory, allocations) with regression tracking.

## Overview
Performance benchmarks ensuring the platform meets the README/spec targets.

Benchmarks must be:
- repeatable
- machine-readable
- diagnostic (logs + environment metadata)

## Benchmarks (README-aligned)
### `fcp bench connector-activate`
- cold start time (p50/p99)
- warm start time (optional)

### `fcp bench invoke-local`
- end-to-end invoke latency when caller/executor are the same node

### `fcp bench invoke-mesh --path=direct|derp`
- end-to-end invoke latency across the mesh for:
  - direct/LAN
  - DERP

### `fcp bench raptorq --size=1mb`
- encode/decode throughput and wall time
- memory high-water mark if available

### `fcp bench secrets --k=3 --n=5`
- secret reconstruction wall time + overhead

### Microbenches (hot primitives)
- canonical CBOR encode/decode
- ObjectId derivation
- CapabilityToken verify
- FCPS frame parse + session MAC verify

## Output
- JSON results with:
  - benchmark name + parameters
  - p50/p90/p99
  - environment (os/arch/cpu)
  - versions + commit hash
- Regression detection policy:
  - start with “report-only”
  - later enforce thresholds once baselines stabilize

## Logging Requirements
Per `flywheel_connectors-1n78.35`, logs must include:
- bench name/params
- sample counts
- any detected outliers

## Acceptance Criteria
- Suite produces stable JSON shapes suitable for snapshot testing.
- Results are reproducible enough to catch real regressions (not noise).

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-h32","title":"STANDARD: Connector Testing Requirements and Quality Gates","description":"# STANDARD: Connector Testing Requirements and Quality Gates

## Goal
Define mandatory connector testing quality gates (mock-only unit tests + E2E compliance runs) so every connector is mechanically verifiable.

## Overview
Defines mandatory testing requirements and quality gates that ALL FCP connectors must pass before release.

This standard is connector-focused and builds on the platform-wide test/logging standard `flywheel_connectors-1n78.35`.

## Recommended Testing Stack (2025-2026)

| Category | Tool | Version | Notes |
|----------|------|---------|-------|
| Unit framework | `cargo test` | built-in | Standard Rust testing |
| Snapshot | `insta` | 1.40+ | JSON + terminal snapshots |
| Property | `proptest` | 0.11+ | Invariant/fuzzy testing |
| Mutation | `cargo-mutants` | 25.1+ | Mutation coverage |
| Fuzzing | `cargo-fuzz` | 0.12+ | Fuzz targets for parsing |
| HTTP mock | `wiremock` | 0.6+ | Async HTTP mocking |
| Assertions | `assert2` | 0.3+ | Better error messages |
| Coverage | `cargo-llvm-cov` | 0.6+ | LLVM-based coverage |
| Security | `cargo-audit` | 0.21+ | Dependency audit |
| Benchmarks | `criterion` | 0.6+ | Statistical benchmarks |

## Unit Test Requirements

### Coverage Thresholds
- Overall: >80% line coverage
- Critical paths: >95% coverage
- Security code: >98% coverage
- **Mutation score**: >70% (cargo-mutants)

### Required Test Categories
- [ ] All connector standard methods (`--manifest`, handshake/describe/introspect/capabilities/configure/invoke/health/shutdown)
- [ ] Configuration validation (valid/invalid)
- [ ] Handshake success/failure scenarios
- [ ] Capability token validation
- [ ] Each operation (success, common errors, edge cases)
- [ ] Rate limit handling
- [ ] Timeout behavior
- [ ] Error recovery
- [ ] **Property tests**: Random inputs for all string/struct parsing

### Mocking Requirements
- NEVER make real API calls in unit tests
- Use wiremock or similar for HTTP mocking
- Mock external service responses
- Test error injection (network, auth, rate limit)

### Property Testing Patterns
```rust
proptest\! {
    #[test]
    fn parse_config_doesnt_panic(input in ".*") {
        // Should never panic, only return Ok/Err
        let _ = ConnectorConfig::parse(&input);
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn roundtrip_serialization(config in arb_connector_config()) {
        let serialized = serde_json::to_string(&config)?;
        let deserialized: ConnectorConfig = serde_json::from_str(&serialized)?;
        prop_assert_eq\!(config, deserialized);
    }
}
```

## Fuzz Testing Requirements (Security-Critical)

### Required Fuzz Targets
Every connector MUST have fuzz targets for:
- [ ] Configuration parsing
- [ ] Protocol message parsing
- [ ] Credential/token handling
- [ ] Event payload parsing

### Fuzz Target Template
```rust
#\![no_main]
use libfuzzer_sys::fuzz_target;

fuzz_target\!(|data: &[u8]| {
    // Should never panic, only return valid error
    let _ = connector::parse_message(data);
});
```

### CI Fuzz Integration
```yaml
- name: Fuzz for 60s per target
  run: cargo +nightly fuzz run --release --sanitizer=address -- -max_total_time=60
```

## Integration Test Requirements
- [ ] Real handshake flow (with test credentials) OR fully deterministic harness
- [ ] Basic operation execution
- [ ] Event subscription lifecycle
- [ ] Reconnection handling

## E2E Test Requirements (Mechanical Compliance)
Every connector MUST pass the platform E2E/compliance harness:
- [ ] Mechanical compliance runner (static + dynamic)
- [ ] Zone binding enforcement
- [ ] Default deny (no cap token → denial + DecisionReceipt)
- [ ] No secret leakage in logs
- [ ] Sandbox/network constraints enforced
- [ ] **Tool descriptor validation** (SEP-1382 compliance)

## Security Testing Requirements

### cargo-audit Gate
```yaml
- name: Security audit
  run: cargo audit --deny warnings --ignore RUSTSEC-XXXX
```

### Secret Leakage Detection
```rust
#[test]
fn no_secrets_in_logs() {
    let logs = capture_connector_logs();
    assert\!(\!logs.contains("api_key="));
    assert\!(\!logs.contains("Authorization:"));
    assert\!(\!logs.contains("Bearer "));
}
```

### Zeroize Verification
```rust
#[test]
fn credentials_are_zeroized_on_drop() {
    let cred = Credential::new("secret");
    let ptr = cred.as_ptr();
    drop(cred);
    // Memory should be zeroed
    unsafe { assert\!(std::slice::from_raw_parts(ptr, 6).iter().all(|&b| b == 0)); }
}
```

## Quality Gates (CI/CD)
1. `cargo check --all-targets` - No errors
2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` - No warnings
3. `cargo fmt --check` - Properly formatted
4. `cargo test` - All tests pass
5. `cargo test --release` - Release tests pass
6. Coverage threshold met (>80%)
7. `cargo audit --deny warnings` - Security scan clean
8. `cargo mutants --timeout-multiplier=2` - Mutation score >70%
9. Fuzz targets run without crash

## Connector Plan Template
For connector planning/bead structure (manifest/provisioning/tests/e2e), use:
- `flywheel_connectors-lszk.5` — STANDARD: Connector Spec Template (FCP2)

## CRITICAL: Detailed Logging Requirements

### All Tests MUST Include Comprehensive Logging

#### Log Levels by Test Phase
- **Setup**: INFO - test initialization, mock configuration
- **Execution**: DEBUG - every significant action
- **Assertions**: INFO - expected vs actual values
- **Teardown**: DEBUG - cleanup operations
- **Errors**: ERROR with full context

#### Required Log Fields (Structured JSON)
Every log entry MUST include:
- `timestamp` - ISO 8601 with milliseconds
- `test_name` - Full test path (module::test_fn)
- `phase` - setup|execute|assert|teardown
- `correlation_id` - Unique per test run
- `duration_ms` - Time since test start
- `operation` - Current operation being tested

When applicable, also include:
- `connector_id`, `zone_id`, `session_id`
- `decision`, `reason_code`, and evidence object IDs for denials

#### Log Format Example
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-01-11T12:00:00.123Z",
  "test_name": "discord::gateway::test_heartbeat_mechanism",
  "phase": "execute",
  "correlation_id": "a1b2c3d4",
  "duration_ms": 45,
  "operation": "send_heartbeat",
  "level": "DEBUG",
  "message": "Sending heartbeat frame",
  "details": {
    "sequence": 42,
    "interval_ms": 41250,
    "last_ack_at": "2026-01-11T11:59:58.500Z"
  }
}
```

#### Test Output Capture
- All tests run with `--nocapture` in CI for full visibility
- Failed tests include complete log history in reports
- Logs retained for 30 days for debugging
- Performance metrics extracted from logs for trending

## Documentation Requirements
- [ ] All public APIs documented
- [ ] AI hints for each operation
- [ ] Configuration schema documented
- [ ] Error codes documented

## Acceptance Criteria
- CI enforces the quality gates.
- The E2E compliance harness rejects non-compliant connectors.
- Mutation testing catches weak test suites.
- Fuzz testing runs on every merge to main.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-h35","title":"fcp.arxiv: Paper search with semantic and keyword modes","description":"# fcp.arxiv: Paper search with semantic and keyword modes (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide high-quality discovery of arXiv papers:
- keyword/fielded search (official arXiv API)
- optional semantic search (requires embeddings + indexed corpus)

## Scope
### Keyword/fielded search
- Support arXiv query syntax with filters:
  - categories
  - author
  - title/abstract
  - date range
- Pagination and sorting.

### Semantic search (optional)
- If enabled, search a local/indexed corpus (e.g., CASS-backed) using embeddings.
- Must be bounded and deterministic.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `arxiv.search.keyword`
- `arxiv.search.semantic` (optional)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned to arXiv API host(s).
- Strict rate limiting to avoid bans.

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `arxiv.search.keyword`
  - `arxiv.search.advanced`
  - Optional: `arxiv.search.semantic`
- Canonical output types:
  - `ArxivSearchResult`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - query parsing/validation
  - pagination
  - rate limiter behavior

## Acceptance criteria
- Search results are stable, paginated, and policy-respecting.

- Enrollment without posture fails closed in required zones.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-jdhaw","title":"[LOW] fcp-graphql GraphqlClient constructors build a fresh HttpClient per call (foot-gun, not a leak)","description":"crates/fcp-graphql/src/client.rs:302-320: both GraphqlClient::new and GraphqlClient::with_config call HttpClientBuilder::new().build() and wrap the result in Arc<HttpClient>. Each constructed GraphqlClient owns its own connection pool.

GraphqlClient implements Clone by sharing the Arc<HttpClient>, so the intended pattern is "construct once, clone everywhere." A caller that instead calls GraphqlClientBuilder::new(url).build() per request will spawn a fresh pool each time; those pools release when the client drops, but HTTP connections held in the pool do not close eagerly.

Not a leak, but the API surface invites the foot-gun. Improvements:

  (a) Accept an Arc<HttpClient> in the builder so callers can share a single pool across many GraphqlClients.
  (b) Add a #[doc] warning on GraphqlClient::new explicitly calling out that each new instance spawns its own pool and recommending Clone.
  (c) Cache-by-endpoint: GraphqlClientBuilder could intern HttpClient instances by (endpoint, tls config, timeout) to share pools across callers. More complex; only if the foot-gun bites in production.

Files: crates/fcp-graphql/src/client.rs:302-320 (constructors), client.rs:193-264 (builder).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-20T08:00:47.361428Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:31:18.403426Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:31:18.402801Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2789,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-jdhaw","author":"jemanuel","text":"Closed across commits a32fa513 (landed the with_http_client builder method, with_shared_http_client constructor, doc warnings on new/with_config, and 3 shared-pool tests -- mis-titled as upp69 but the diff carried all jdhaw changes too) + ab9aa968 (adds parity test: with_shared_http_client and builder.with_http_client must produce clients that share an Arc<HttpClient> when handed the same one).  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-w82c","title":"[FCP2] Long-Running Operation Progress: Real-Time Feedback","description":"# [FCP2] Long-Running Operation Progress: Real-Time Feedback

## Goal
Enable agents to receive progress updates for long-running operations without blocking or polling.

## Problem
Some connector operations take significant time:
- Large file uploads/downloads
- Batch API calls
- Data migrations
- Complex queries

Agents currently have no visibility into progress - they either block waiting or timeout without knowing how close the operation was to completion.

## Solution: Progress Streaming

### Request Format
```jsonc
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 1,
  "method": "tools/invoke",
  "params": {
    "tool": "fcp.storage.upload",
    "input": {
      "path": "/large-file.tar.gz",
      "destination": "s3://bucket/path"
    },
    "options": {
      "stream_progress": true,  // Request progress updates
      "progress_interval_ms": 500  // Min interval between updates
    }
  }
}
```

### Progress Notification Format
When `stream_progress: true`, the host sends JSON-RPC notifications:

```jsonc
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "method": "progress/update",
  "params": {
    "operation_id": "op_abc123",
    "request_id": 1,  // Correlates to original request
    "progress": {
      "phase": "uploading",  // Current phase name
      "current": 45000000,   // Current progress (bytes, items, etc.)
      "total": 100000000,    // Total expected (if known)
      "unit": "bytes",       // Unit of measurement
      "percentage": 45.0,    // Computed percentage
      "rate": 5000000,       // Rate per second (if applicable)
      "eta_ms": 11000,       // Estimated time remaining
      "message": "Uploading chunk 9 of 20"  // Human-readable status
    },
    "timestamp": "2026-01-22T18:00:00Z"
  }
}
```

### Phase Transitions
```jsonc
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "method": "progress/phase",
  "params": {
    "operation_id": "op_abc123",
    "request_id": 1,
    "from_phase": "preparing",
    "to_phase": "uploading",
    "phases_remaining": ["verifying", "completing"],
    "timestamp": "2026-01-22T18:00:00Z"
  }
}
```

### Implementation

```rust
pub struct ProgressTracker {
    operation_id: OperationId,
    request_id: RequestId,
    sender: mpsc::Sender<ProgressNotification>,
    interval: Duration,
    last_sent: Instant,
}

impl ProgressTracker {
    /// Report progress (throttled by interval)
    pub async fn report(&mut self, progress: Progress) -> Result<(), FcpError> {
        if self.last_sent.elapsed() >= self.interval {
            let notification = ProgressNotification {
                operation_id: self.operation_id.clone(),
                request_id: self.request_id,
                progress,
                timestamp: Utc::now(),
            };
            self.sender.send(notification).await?;
            self.last_sent = Instant::now();
        }
        Ok(())
    }

    /// Report phase transition (always sent)
    pub async fn transition(&mut self, from: &str, to: &str, remaining: &[&str]) -> Result<(), FcpError> {
        let notification = PhaseTransition {
            operation_id: self.operation_id.clone(),
            request_id: self.request_id,
            from_phase: from.to_string(),
            to_phase: to.to_string(),
            phases_remaining: remaining.iter().map(|s| s.to_string()).collect(),
            timestamp: Utc::now(),
        };
        self.sender.send(notification.into()).await?;
        Ok(())
    }
}

pub struct Progress {
    pub phase: String,
    pub current: u64,
    pub total: Option<u64>,
    pub unit: ProgressUnit,
    pub rate: Option<u64>,
    pub message: Option<String>,
}

pub enum ProgressUnit {
    Bytes,
    Items,
    Requests,
    Rows,
    Custom(String),
}
```

### Connector SDK Support

```rust
#[fcp_tool(streaming = true)]
async fn upload_file(
    ctx: &ToolContext,
    input: UploadInput,
    progress: ProgressTracker,  // Injected if stream_progress=true
) -> Result<UploadOutput, FcpError> {
    progress.transition("preparing", "uploading", &["verifying"]).await?;

    let mut uploaded = 0u64;
    for chunk in file.chunks(CHUNK_SIZE) {
        upload_chunk(chunk).await?;
        uploaded += chunk.len() as u64;
        progress.report(Progress {
            phase: "uploading".to_string(),
            current: uploaded,
            total: Some(file_size),
            unit: ProgressUnit::Bytes,
            rate: Some(calculate_rate()),
            message: Some(format!("Uploaded {} of {}", uploaded, file_size)),
        }).await?;
    }

    progress.transition("uploading", "verifying", &[]).await?;
    verify_upload().await?;

    Ok(UploadOutput { bytes_uploaded: uploaded })
}
```

### Tests

```rust
#[tokio::test]
async fn test_progress_streaming_basic() {
    let harness = TestHarness::new().await;
    let (tx, mut rx) = mpsc::channel(100);

    harness.invoke_with_progress("test.slow_operation", json!({}), tx).await;

    let mut progress_updates = vec![];
    while let Some(update) = rx.recv().await {
        progress_updates.push(update);
    }

    assert!(progress_updates.len() > 0);
    // Progress should be monotonically increasing
    for window in progress_updates.windows(2) {
        assert!(window[1].progress.percentage >= window[0].progress.percentage);
    }
}
```

- [ ] Progress notifications are throttled by configurable interval
- [ ] Phase transitions are always sent (not throttled)
- [ ] Progress includes rate and ETA when calculable
- [ ] Works with both deterministic and indeterminate progress
- [ ] Progress stops immediately on cancellation

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-w94","title":"fcp.airtable: View filtering and sorting support","description":"# fcp.airtable: View Filtering and Sorting Support

## Goal
Support Airtable views as a first-class query primitive (views encode filters/sorts that humans already curate).

## External surface (NetworkConstraints)
Default deny.
- Network egress MUST be restricted per-operation via manifest `NetworkConstraints`.
- Allow only the configured service host(s); HTTPS-only where applicable; deny redirects by default.
- Bound timeouts + response sizes; treat all remote responses as untrusted input; never leak secrets/PII in logs or errors.

## Zone model (mechanical)

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

Recommended deployment pattern: run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies.

## Scope
Operations (representative):
- `airtable.views.list`
- `airtable.views.get`
- `airtable.views.records` (list records through a view)

## Key requirements
- Capability gated (`airtable.records.read` and/or `airtable.views.read`).
- Views must be treated as “query presets”:
  - do not silently broaden access beyond what the view returns
  - enforce field selection and page size bounds
- Sorting:
  - preserve deterministic ordering when paginating
- Filter formulas:
  - validate/escape inputs to prevent injection into filter expressions

## Tests
- Mock-only tests cover:
  - pagination stability under view sorting
  - malformed view configs
  - permission failures and rate limits

## Acceptance Criteria
- Agents can reliably paginate view results without duplicates.
- Filter and sort behavior matches Airtable semantics.
","notes":"Implemented first-class Airtable view primitives and tightened list query validation.

Delivered:
- added airtable.list_views, airtable.get_view, and airtable.list_view_records in connector introspection/invoke path
- list_records now resolves table/view/field selectors against schema and validates filter, bounds, and sort inputs before making Airtable requests
- manifest metadata and operation pools updated for the new view operations
- integration coverage added for view lookup, ambiguous view names, view-record pagination via offset, and filter/sort/view query encoding

Verification:
- rch exec -- cargo fmt --package fcp-airtable -- --check
- rch exec -- cargo check -p fcp-airtable --all-targets --target-dir .target-airtable-w94

+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-wawhf","title":"fuzz/capability: target only parses COSE/CWT and drops results","description":"File: fuzz/fuzz_targets/capability.rs:11-18

Finding: the fuzz body only calls `CoseToken::from_cbor(data)` and `CwtClaims::from_cbor(data)` and discards both results. That catches panics, but it does not assert any semantic invariant when parsing succeeds.

Why this matters: successful parses can still violate token/claims consistency contracts without the fuzz target noticing.

Suggested follow-up: when both parses succeed, re-encode or cross-check key claims invariants (for example deterministic CBOR round-trip or claim-shape sanity) instead of parse-and-drop only.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:19:40.562062Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:19:40.562062Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-wd6ei","title":"[HIGH] fcp-graphql dedup map leaks entries on caller cancel/panic","description":"crates/fcp-graphql/src/client.rs:519-538 (execute_bytes) implements in-flight request dedup as:

    let mut guard = state.inner.lock().await;
    if let Some(shared) = guard.get(&key).cloned() {
        drop(guard);
        return shared.await;
    }
    let future = async move { client.send_with_retry(payload, idempotent).await }
        .boxed()
        .shared();
    guard.insert(key, future.clone());
    drop(guard);
    let result = future.await;
    state.inner.lock().await.remove(&key); // <-- only on non-cancel non-panic completion
    return result;

Failure modes:

1. Caller cancel: if the outer task is cancelled or dropped at the `future.await` suspension point, the removal line is skipped. The Shared handle stays in `state.inner` indefinitely.

2. Panic: same -- the `remove` line is not unwind-safe.

3. Result caching beyond in-flight: once Shared has resolved, subsequent same-body callers get the cached result forever (not just while in-flight). A response cached for Query X at T is served to all future duplicates of X, potentially hours later. dedup-in-flight semantics are silently promoted to "dedup forever".

Compounding: memory usage grows monotonically with the number of unique request bodies that the owning caller cancelled on. No TTL, no eviction, no size cap.

Fix shape: RAII guard that removes the entry on drop, unconditionally:

    struct DedupGuard { state: Arc<DedupState>, key: u64 }
    impl Drop for DedupGuard { fn drop(&mut self) { /* blocking remove or spawn remove */ } }

Or use a DashMap<u64, Weak<Shared>> so stale entries self-clean when all Shared handles drop.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-weh1v","title":"GCP: implement service account JWT signing or gate with explicit error","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:07:47.545621Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-25T04:53:03.751662Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T04:53:03.751484Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["connectors","hardening","placeholders"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-weh1v","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-ohw06","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:03.521810Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1943,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-weh1v","author":"jemanuel","text":"AFFECTED FILE: connectors/gcp/src/types.rs:53-54

CURRENT STATE:
The GcpAuth::ServiceAccount variant's token() method returns the private_key field directly as a bearer token placeholder. Comment says 'real JWT signing would be added later'. This means service_account auth mode silently sends a raw private key as a Bearer token to Google APIs — which is both a security risk (key leakage in transit) and functionally broken (Google rejects non-JWT bearer tokens).

The access_token and credential_id auth modes work correctly. Only service_account is affected.

FIX:
1. Implement proper JWT signing for service_account mode:
   - Build JWT header: {"alg":"RS256","typ":"JWT"}
   - Build JWT claims: {"iss":service_account_email, "scope":scopes, "aud":"https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token", "iat":now, "exp":now+3600}
   - Sign with RS256 using the private key
   - Exchange signed JWT for access token via https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token
2. Alternatively, gate service_account mode with an explicit error: 'GCP service_account JWT signing is not yet implemented. Use access_token or credential_id mode.'
3. The fcp-google-discovery crate already handles Google auth via GoogleAuthSelection/GoogleMaterializedAuth — consider whether GCP connector should use that substrate instead of its own auth.

RECOMMENDATION: Gate with explicit error (option 2) as an immediate fix, then evaluate whether the GCP connector should migrate to the shared Google auth substrate (option 3) as a follow-up.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- service_account mode either produces valid JWTs or returns a clear error (no raw private key in Bearer header)
- doctor() reports auth mode and warns if service_account is selected without JWT support
- Tests verify the error path for service_account mode","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:03Z"}]}
